coredata.dc:title: Experimental and DFT studies on corrosion protection performance of epoxy/graphene quantum dots@TiO2 nanotubes coatings
coredata.dc:creator: Ji, Xiaohong
Seif, Abdolvahab
Duan, Jizhou
Rashidi, Alimorad
Zhou, Ziyang
Pourhashem, Sepideh
Mirzaee, Majid

Hou, Baorong
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, the nanohybrids of graphene quantum dots and TiO2 nanotubes (GQD@TNT) with different GQD:TNT weight ratios of 1:0.5, 1:1, and 1:2 are synthesized which are abbreviated as H (1:0.5), H (1:1), and H (1:2), respectively, and further modified with 3-(aminopropyl) triethoxysilane. Then, the silane-modified nanohybrids are loaded at 0.3 wt% in the solvent-borne epoxy matrix to achieve anti-corrosion nanocomposite coatings on the steel substrates. The results reveal that the coatings loaded with silane-modified H (1:1) more effectively enhance the corrosion protection efficiency of the polymer coatings, which has a low-frequency impedance modulus of 5.64 × 109 Ω·cm2 even after 90 days of immersion in the 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. Moreover, the density functional theory (DFT) method is performed to explore the energetics and the interaction mechanisms at each interface. The results imply that the strong adhesions observed at each interface would twist and increase the diffusion path of the corrosive agents, and consequently, retard the electrochemical corrosion reactions at the surface of the metal. Therefore, the proposed strategies could be successfully applied for the development of long-term anti-corrosion coatings.

The points and lines represent the original experimental data and their corresponding fitting results, respectively.

The optimized geometry of (a) TiO2-GQD, (b) GQD-silane, and (c) silane-epoxy resin. A TiO2 surface made from anatase (101) crystal planar (titanium atoms are in pale gray, oxygen atoms are in red, carbon atom are in bold gray, and hydrogen atoms are in white.) The equilibrium distances (Å) and the electrostatic charges |e| of the interacting atoms are reported by +/ - signs.

head.ce:title: Experimental and DFT studies on corrosion protection performance of epoxy/graphene quantum dots@TiO2 nanotubes coatings

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, the nanohybrids of graphene quantum dots and TiO2 nanotubes (GQD@TNT) with different GQD:TNT weight ratios of 1:0.5, 1:1, and 1:2 are synthesized which are abbreviated as H (1:0.5), H (1:1), and H (1:2), respectively, and further modified with 3-(aminopropyl) triethoxysilane. Then, the silane-modified nanohybrids are loaded at 0.3 wt% in the solvent-borne epoxy matrix to achieve anti-corrosion nanocomposite coatings on the steel substrates. The results reveal that the coatings loaded with silane-modified H (1:1) more effectively enhance the corrosion protection efficiency of the polymer coatings, which has a low-frequency impedance modulus of 5.64 × 109 Ω·cm2 even after 90 days of immersion in the 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. Moreover, the density functional theory (DFT) method is performed to explore the energetics and the interaction mechanisms at each interface. The results imply that the strong adhesions observed at each interface would twist and increase the diffusion path of the corrosive agents, and consequently, retard the electrochemical corrosion reactions at the surface of the metal. Therefore, the proposed strategies could be successfully applied for the development of long-term anti-corrosion coatings.

ce:section.ce:para: Corrosion is considered a natural electrochemical phenomenon that gradually transforms the metals or alloys into more stable chemical compounds (such as metal oxides, metal hydroxides, metal carbonates, metal chlorides or sulfides) due to the interactions of the metallic materials with their surroundings [1–3]. Since corrosion has enormous economic costs and safety hazards for industries, different approaches based on material selection, barrier coatings, corrosion inhibitors, and cathodic/anodic corrosion protection techniques have been considered for decreasing the corrosion rate of the metallic structures [4,5].

In this regard, polymer coatings are appropriate candidates to retard the corrosion phenomenon, especially in harsh marine environments [6,7]. According to ISO 12944, a 3-layer coating system (consisting of zinc-rich epoxy as a primer layer, epoxy as an intermediate layer, and polyurethane as top layer) is typically selected for application in the marine atmosphere to achieve the multi-purpose of corrosion resistance, erosion resistance, anti-icing, and UV-resistance. The coatings used in splash and tidal zone are two or three epoxy-based coatings with a polyurethane top layer, to combat both atmospheric degradation and corrosion reactions. The submerged structures are coated with two or three layers of epoxy-based coatings to achieve corrosion, fouling, and wear resistance [8].

As mentioned above, epoxy coatings are widely applied as anti-corrosion coatings on the metallic structures in the marine environments. The epoxy coatings could be categorized as solvent-borne and water-based epoxies, in which organic solvents and water are the diluting media for the binders, pigments, and additives in these polymer systems, respectively [9,10]. However, although the solvent-borne epoxy coatings are traditionally consumed in the marine environments, the cracks and micro-pores are created in the coating matrix during the curing step due to solvent evaporation; therefore, the corrosive electrolyte easily penetrate within the matrix of the coating and degrade the corrosion protection behavior [11,12].

Accordingly, polymer nanocomposites are introduced to achieve enhanced corrosion resistance [13–15], weathering resistance [16–18], anti-fouling performance [19–22], mechanical strength [23–25], and flame retardant properties [26–28]. Meanwhile, amongst various nano-metal oxides loaded as nanofillers in the polymer matrix, nano-TiO2 with unique properties of high chemical stability, corrosion resistance, and non-toxicity is a suitable candidate for developing polymer coatings with high corrosion protection efficiency [29–33]. For example, Shafaamri et al. [34] studied the role of the TiO2 wt.% (0.1, 0.2, and 0.3 wt%) on the corrosion resistance of the epoxy/polydimethylsiloxane coatings, and their results confirmed that the nanocomposite coatings loaded with 0.1 wt% TiO2 nanoparticles have improved UV absorbance, high water contact angle, low surface roughness, and excellent barrier resistance in comparison to the neat epoxy and the epoxy/polydimethylsiloxane coatings. Abd El-Lateef et al. [35] utilized the advantages of different metal oxides (i.e. ZnO, TiO2, and ZnO-TiO2) in the organic matrix of the polyvinyl chloride (PVC) to enhance the corrosion resistance of the carbon steel in 0.5 M HCl + 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. Their results revealed that the corrosion resistance of the nanocomposite coatings increases as follows: ZnO/PVC < TiO2/PVC < ZnO-TiO2/PVC. Fadl et al. [36] investigated the properties of polydimethylamino siloxane/TiO2 as a multifunctional modifier for improving the self-healing, corrosion resistance, UV immovability, thermal and electrical stability, and mechanical properties of the polyamine-cured epoxy coatings, indicating that the nano-linkages between the transition metal and the organic matrix (i.e. Ti-O-C, Ti-O-, O-Ti-O, Si-O-Si, and Si-O) play an important role in improving the properties of the coatings.

Furthermore, graphene quantum dots (GQD) as zero-dimensional graphene based nanomaterials with particle size below 100 nm and thickness less than 10 layers [37–39], have been recently used as corrosion inhibitors in the corrosive electrolyte and anti-corrosion nanofillers in the polymer coatings [39,40]. As an example, Pourhashem et al. [41,42] considered the corrosion protection properties of the epoxy coatings loaded with GQD, and compared it with the performance of the epoxy/graphene nanocomposite coatings; proving that although both GQD and graphene nanosheets could improve the barrier resistance of the epoxy coatings, the epoxy/GQD nanocomposite coatings have higher corrosion resistance which is attributed to the better dispersion quality of the GQD within the polymer matrix through the presence of the functional groups on the GQD surface. Ramezanzadeh et al. [43] used polyaniline modified graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) to enhance the corrosion resistance of the epoxy coatings; indicating that the polyaniline modified GOQDs are able to uniformly disperse in the coating matrix, fill the pores of the coating, and increase the diffusion way of the corrosive agents. Moreover, Jiang et al. [44] electrodeposited the N-doped GQD/polydimethyltrimethoxysiloxane nanocomposite coating on the magnesium alloy. Their investigations confirmed that the developed coating system has high corrosion resistance in the 3.5 wt% NaCl solution due to the presence of the strong chemical bonds between the N-doped GQD and the polydimethyltrimethoxysiloxane coating.

Therefore, in this research, our main goal is to utilize the advantages of both GQD and nano-TiO2 for increasing the barrier resistance of the solvent-borne epoxy coatings. Since the nanohybrids are regarded as a new approach for utilizing the merits of different nanomaterials in a unique and uniform structure [45–47], the nanohybrids of the GQD and the nano-TiO2 are synthesized through decorating the surface of the TiO2 nanotubes (TNT) with GQD; and then, the GQD@TNT nanohybrids are chemically modified with silane coupling agents to achieve uniform distribution of the nanohybrids in the epoxy matrix [48]. In addition, the density functional theory (DFT) method is used to study the energetics and the interaction mechanisms at all the interfaces. To the best of our knowledge, there is no report on the application of the silane-modified GQD@TNT nanohybrids in the solvent-borne epoxy coatings. Meanwhile, the synergistic effects of the GQD and the TNT in the epoxy are compared with that of the GQD and the TNT as single nanomaterials in the coatings.

Titanium tetraisopropoxide (TTIP, 97%), isopropanol, 3-(aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES, 98%), citric acid, and urea with analytical grade are purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Sodium hydroxide, nitric acid, hydrochloric acid (37%), acetone, toluene, and ethanol (≥99.7%) are supplied from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent (China). Epoxy resin (E44), its amine hardener (T31), and coating thinner are bought from Zhenjiang Danbao Resin Co., Ltd., China. Q235 carbon steels (with wt.% of C = 0.18%, Si = 0.28%, Mn = 0.55%, S = 0.035%, P = 0.04%, and Fe = balance) with three different sizes (including 10 mm × 10 mm × 10 mm, 50 mm × 70 mm × 3 mm, and 70 mm × 150 mm × 3 mm) are used as metallic substrate for coating; these metallic substrates are polished with 400 and 1000 grit SiC paper, and, cleaned by ethanol and acetone.

The details about the synthesis method of the TNT is described in our previous works [49]. The GQD is prepared through mixing 2.94 g citric acid, 2.52 g urea, and 70 mL DI water at room temperature for 30 min through magnetic stirring, followed by hydrothermal reaction in a Teflon-lined autoclave with high-quality stainless steel jacket at 160 °C for 4 h. The solution product of the hydrothermal reaction is dried in an oven at 80 °C. The dried product is finally grinded in an agar mortar to have a fine powder of GQD.

In the next step, the GQD@TNT nanohybrids are synthesized through mixing different weight ratios of GQD to TNT (i.e. GQD:TNT) including 1:0.5, 1:1, and 1:2, and the prepared nanohybrids are abbreviated as H (1:0.5), H (1:1), and H (1:2), respectively. According to the selected weight ratio, the required amounts of the GQD and the TNT are mixed together in DI water through stirring for 2 h at room temperature; for example, the H (1:1) is prepared by mixing 1 g GQD and 1 g TNT in 70 mL DI water by using magnetic stirring at room temperature for 2 h. Then, the reaction is followed by hydrothermal treatment in a Teflon-lined autoclave with a high-quality stainless steel jacket at 150 ℃ for 4 h. The product of the hydrothermal reaction is dried in an oven at 80 °C, and finally, grinded in an agar mortar.

The synthesized nanomaterials including GQD, TNT, H (1:0.5), H (1:1), and H (1:2) are modified with APTES, abbreviated as S-GQD, S-TNT, S-H (1:0.5), S-H (1:1), and S-H (1:2), respectively. Accordingly, 0.2 g nanomaterial is dispersed in 400 mL DI water by 30 min magnetic stirring at room temperature, followed by 60 min bath ultrasonic treatment. In another container, 2.2 g APTES is dissolved in 50 mL toluene via 30 min stirring at room temperature. Then, the APTES solution is added into the dispersion of the nanomaterials in DI water, and refluxed at 80 °C for 24 h under continuous stirring. The product of the reflux reaction is the silane-modified nanomaterial which is washed with water and ethanol by using centrifuge at 9000 rpm for 5 min. Fig. S1 (in Supplementary Data) schematically shows the methods used for the synthesis of the GQD@TNT nanohybrids and their silane modification.

The nanocomposite epoxy coatings are prepared by embedding 0.3 wt% silane-modified nanomaterials including S-GQD, S-TNT, S-H (1:0.5), S-H (1:1), and S-H (1:2) into the solvent-borne epoxy coating, regarded as EP/S-GQD, EP/S-TNT, EP/S-H (1:0.5), EP/S-H (1:1), and EP/S-H (1:2), respectively. Accordingly, 0.36 g silane-modified nanomaterial is homogeneously dispersed in 30 mL thinner by using bath ultrasonic treatment for 2 h. Then, the uniform dispersion of the nanomaterial/thinner is added into 100 g epoxy resin (which is pre-heated at 70 °C to decrease its viscosity and improve the distribution of the nanomaterials in the epoxy resin), followed by 1 h stirring at 70 °C. Then, the heater is turned-off to cool down to room temperature, while the mixture is stirred vigorously. After reaching to room temperature, 20 g hardener is added into the mixture and stirred for 15 min at room temperature. Next, the mixture is held in a steady state without stirring to remove the bubbles, followed by painting on the pre-cleaned Q235 steel substrates, curing at room temperature for 3 days, and curing in an oven at 80 °C for 90 min. The measurement of the coating thickness is done by a digital coating thickness gauge (GTS8202, Guangzhou GuoOu Electronic Technology Co.). The coatings with thickness of 60 ± 10 μm are used for further characterization.

The pure epoxy coatings (abbreviated as EP) are prepared through mixing stoichiometric amounts of the epoxy resin and the hardener (which is 100 g epoxy resin and 20 g hardener), diluting with 30 mL thinner, bubble removing, painting on pre-cleaned Q235 steel substrates, curing at room temperature for 3 days, and finally, curing in an oven at 80 °C for 90 min.

The morphology of the synthesized nanomaterials is assessed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, HT7700), while their structure is considered by different techniques including X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD, Rigaku Ultima IV, Japan) at 2θ scan range of 10-80° with 10°/min scanning speed, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet iS10) in 4000–400 cm−1 wavelength, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Escalab 250Xi) with Al Kα (1486.68 eV) as X-ray source.

The pull-off adhesion test (DeFelsko, PosiTest, automatic) is performed according to ASTM D4541 to measure the adhesion strength of the coatings to the substrates. The corrosion protection efficiency of the coatings is considered by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and salt spray test. The EIS measurements are recorded by using a Potentiostat/Galvanostat (PGSTAT302N, Metrohm Autolab, Utrecht, Netherlands) at steady state open circuit potential (OCP) in 10-2–105 Hz frequency range with 10 mV amplitude. The coated sample, saturated Hg/Hg2Cl2 electrode, and Pt wire are used as working, reference, and counter electrodes in the electrochemical cell, respectively. Moreover, the salt spray test is used to visually inspect the corrosion resistance of the prepared coatings. The salt spray test is carried out according to ASTM B-117, and the experimental parameters in the salt fog chamber are 5 wt% NaCl solution, 100% relative humidity, and 1 min salt spraying for every 6 min.

All the spin-polarized DFT calculations are performed using the DMol3 program [50]. The geometry optimization and energy calculation of each complex are carried out using the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with the Perdew–Burke–Ernzernhof (PBE) functional [51]. Grimme dispersion corrected density functional theory (DFT-D) has been used to take into account the Van der Waals forces in the adsorption energies [52]. A double numerical plus polarization (DNP) basis set is adopted for all the atoms. The supercell size of 3 × 4 with a vacuum slab of 15 Å is considered for the simulation. Respecting the experimental results, the (101) anatase TiO2 slab is selected for our simulations. Brillouin zone integration for the energy calculations is performed using a Gamma point. Finally, the adsorption energy (Eads) of each adsorbate over the adsorbent is obtained by the following equation:
(1)
E ads
=
E
adsorbate + adsorbent
-
(
E adsorbate
+
E adsorbent
)
where Eadsorbate+adsorbent is the total energy of the adsorbent-adsorbate complexes and Eadsorbate (Eadsorbent) is the energy of the adsorbate (adsorbent).

The morphology of the synthesized nanomaterials is shown in Fig. 1 . The TEM image in Fig. 1(a) reveals that the GQD with spherical particles in the range of less than 20 nm are prepared, while the HR-TEM image of the GQD (Fig. 1(b)) indicates the hexagonal network of the graphene with ∼ 0.32 nm in-plane lattice parameter. The TEM image of the TNT in Fig. 1(c) represents that the nanotubes with diameter of less than 10 nm and length of several hundred nanometers are successfully prepared. Moreover, the TEM images in Fig. 1(d-f) show the morphology of the nanohybrids with different GQD:TNT weight ratios, indicating that the H (1:1) has the most uniform dispersion of the TNT and the GQD. In these nanohybrids, although there is a contrast and the wall of the TNT could be distinguished, the GQD are not observed clearly and they have appeared as a dark background between the TNT; which is maybe due to the agglomeration of the small-sized GQD on the TNT [53–55].

Fig. 2 shows the XRD patterns of the synthesized nanomaterials. The XRD pattern of the TNT in Fig. 2(a) confirms that the TNT is mainly composed of anatase phase (JCPDS 21–1272), while brookite phase (JCPDS # 29–1360) has appeared as a negligible phase [32,49]. According to Fig. 2(b), the GQD show a peak at 2θ = 29.3° with d-spacing of about 0.31 nm related to the (002) diffraction plane of graphite, which indicates the graphitic-like structure of the GQD [41,56]. Furthermore, the patterns of the nanohybrids in Fig. 2(c) show all the characteristic peaks of the TNT and the GQD with the same peak position. The differences observed in peak strength are related to the content of the nanomaterials within the nanohybrids, approving that the nanohybrid materials are successfully synthesized [57].

The surface chemical composition of the synthesized H (1:1) and S-H (1:1) is investigated by XPS. The XPS survey scan is shown in Fig. 3 (a), revealing that the H (1:1) is mainly composed of Ti, O, N, and C, while the surface elemental composition of the S-H (1:1) is composed of Si, C, N, and O. The disappearance of Ti element in the survey scan of the S-H (1:1) sample is due to the high weight ratio of the APTES to the GQD@TNT in this sample. Indeed, the high amounts of the APTES used in the synthesis of the silane-modified nanohybrid has led to the full coverage of the S-H (1:1) with the APTES elements, which has been previously observed by Salantiu et al. [58]. For H (1:1) sample, the Ti 2p high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 3(b)) is deconvoluted into two main peaks at 459 and 464.6 eV, attributed to the Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, respectively [59–61]. The O 1 s high-resolution spectrum of the H (1:1) in Fig. 3(c) is decomposed to peaks located at 530.1 eV (Ti-O), 531.2 eV (C-O-Ti), 532.2 eV (C-O-Ti), and 535.1 eV (C-OH bonds) [54,62,63]. The presence of C-O-Ti bonds indicate that the TNT and the GQD are covalently bonded through oxygen atom [64]. After silane modification, the O 1 s high-resolution scan of the S-H (1:1) in Fig. 3(c) is decomposed to three peaks of 531.2, 532.0, and 532.3 eV, all assigned to the C-O-Ti bonds within the structure [60]. Fig. 3(d) represents the C 1 s high-resolution spectra of the H (1:1) and S-H (1:1). The C 1 s high-resolution spectra of the H (1:1) could be fitted to peaks at 284.5, 285.2, 286.7, and 288.3 eV, assigned to the C = C (honeycomb structure), C-N, C = O, and COOH, respectively [54,59,62]; while the C 1 s high-resolution spectrum of the S-H (1:1) is deconvoluted to two peaks at 284.8 and 285.6 eV related to the C = C and C-N, respectively [59]. Moreover, the N 1 s high-resolution scans of H (1:1) and S-H (1:1) are depicted in Fig. 3(e), and both samples show almost similar deconvoluted peak positions. The N 1 s high-resolution spectrum of the H (1:1) is decomposed to peaks at 399.5, 400.2, and 401.2 eV, while the N 1 s high-resolution spectrum of the S-H (1:1) is decomposed to peaks at 399.25, 400.0, and 401.2 eV; these peaks are attributed to the NH2 (pyrrolic N), N–H, and N-C bonds, respectively [54,59]. The Si 2p element has appeared in the S-H (1:1) sample due to modifying the nanohybrid with the APTES, and as Fig. 3(f) shows, this peak is deconvoluted to a peak at 102.4 eV (Si-O bond) [49]. The XPS results about the H (1:1) sample indicate that the TNT and GQD are bonded together through the conjunction of Ti-O-C bond between the Ti-O of the TNT and the C-O of the GQD [59]. Besides, in the case of S-H (1:1), the silane agent is grafted on the nanohybrid through the C-O bonds of the GQD. Besides, the FTIR spectra of the synthesized nanomaterials before and after silane modification are presented in Fig. S2 (in Supplementary Data) to further consider their chemical reactions.

It should be mentioned that since the adhesion strength of the polymer coatings to the metallic structures is an important parameter in corrosion protection, the average pull-off adhesion strength of the coatings are presented in Fig. S3 and discussed in Supplementary Data.

The variation of the OCP values for the EP and the nanocomposite coatings are depicted in Fig. S4. Besides, the corrosion resistance of the prepared coatings are studied by EIS, and the derived Nyquist, Bode, and Phase plots of the coated steel substrates during immersion time in the 3.5 wt% NaCl solution are presented in Figs. 4, 5, and 6
, respectively. In the case of Nyquist plots, samples with bigger capacitance arcs provide better corrosion resistance. However, the capacitance arc of the Nyquist diagram decreases gradually by extending the immersion time, indicating that the anti-corrosion effect of the coating decreases with increasing the immersion time. The differences in the arc radius for different samples is more obvious by increasing the time of the immersion. In this regard, the capacitance arcs of the EP coating (Fig. 4(a)) and the nanocomposite coatings loaded with solo-nanofillers (Fig. 4(b and c)) are very small after 90 days of immersion in the 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, and the coatings have shown a second time constant during the immersion time; indicating that the corrosion reactions have occurred at the coating/steel interface. Furthermore, the Nyquist plots of the coatings containing the nanohybrids (Fig. 4(d-f)) have shown a negligible difference in the radius of the capacitance arc by prolonging the immersion time, especially in the case of the EP/S-H (1:1) sample (Fig. 4(e)) which has revealed only one time constant after being soaked for 90 days. The results prove that that the nanohybrids are the most appropriate candidates to enhance the service-life of the epoxy coating in the corrosive media, compared to the solo-nanofillers (GQD and TNT).

Moreover, the Bode plots are used to consider the variation of the impedance modules of the coated steel substrates during immersion in the 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The Bode plots (in Fig. 5) clearly represent the capacitive behavior in high-frequency range and resistive behavior in low-frequency range. The high-frequency region reflects the defects and capacitance response of the coating, while the low-frequency region reflects the corrosion response of the steel substrate [65]. The low-frequency impedance modulus of the samples (|Z|0.01Hz) reflects the anti-corrosion performance of the coating system, and the coating systems with higher |Z|0.01Hz values could provide higher corrosion protection performance. It is usually considered that the coatings with |Z|0.01Hz values of less than 108 Ω·cm2, would not have good corrosion resistance [66]. In the case of the EP sample (Fig. 5(a)), the |Z|0.01Hz value of 9.04 × 108 Ω·cm2 is recorded in the first day of immersion which indicates that the EP sample has barrier protection. However, the |Z|0.01Hz value of the EP sample decrease significantly with prolonging the immersion time and it reaches to 2.20 × 104 Ω·cm2 after 90 days of immersion, demonstrating that the EP sample has failed completely and has lost its corrosion protection capability. For the EP/S-GQD sample (Fig. 5(b)) and EP/S-TNT sample (Fig. 5(c)), the |Z|0.01Hz values decreased to 1.39 × 106 Ω·cm2 and 3.20 × 106 Ω·cm2 after 90 days of immersion, respectively. The |Z|0.01Hz values of the EP/S-GQD and EP/S-TNT nanocomposite coatings are higher than the EP sample during the immersion time, revealing that both S-GQD and S-TNT could enhance the barrier performance of the epoxy matrix, and meanwhile, the EP/S-TNT has somehow better performance than the EP/S-GQD. In the case of nanocomposite coatings loaded with nanohybrids (according to Fig. 5(d-f)), the initial |Z|0.01Hz values of the EP/S-H (1:0.5), EP/S-H (1:1), and EP/S-H (1:2) samples are 9.89 × 1010 Ω·cm2, 1.15 × 1011 Ω·cm2, and 5.69 × 109 Ω·cm2, which declined to 4.50 × 108 Ω·cm2, 5.64 × 109 Ω·cm2, and 3.35 × 108 Ω·cm2 after 90 days of immersion in the 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, respectively. The high |Z|0.01Hz values of the nanocomposite samples containing nanohybrids clarify the efficient role of the nanohybrids in enhancing the barrier effects of the epoxy matrix. Meanwhile, the EP/S-H (1:1) sample has provided the highest corrosion resistance and barrier performance, which is also consistent with the results of the OCP measurements. The variation of the |Z|0.01Hz values for the coatings are presented in Fig. S5.

According to the descriptions related to the Phase plots (Fig. 6), when the coated samples show two time constants, the corrosive species have reached the steel surface at the immersion time. The Phase plots with two time constants have appeared in the case of EP (Fig. 6(a)), EP/S-GQD (Fig. 6(b)), and EP/S-TNT (Fig. 6(c)) samples by increasing immersion time, revealing that the corrosion has occurred on the surface of the steel substrate. However, only one time constant is detected in Phase plots of the nanocomposite coatings loaded with nanohybrids (Fig. 6(d-f)), confirming that these nanocomposite coatings can effectively prevent the penetration of the corrosive media to the metal/coating interface.

Moreover, EIS results are fitted by equivalent electrical circuits (in Fig. S6) and the results of the coating resistance (Rc) and charge transfer resistance (Rct) for the prepared coating systems during immersion in the 3.5 wt% NaCl solution are presented in Fig. S7. These results are discussed in Supplementary Data.

The long-term anti-corrosion performance of the coatings is evaluated by salt spray test, and the artificial scratches are made on the coating surface with a scalpel (before the salt spray test) to accelerate the corrosion phenomenon. Moreover, the SEM and EDS analysis are used to study the corrosion products. For SEM/EDS analysis, the coatings on the surface of the substrate are removed, followed by cleaning with ethanol. Fig. 7 shows the digital photographs of the coatings after 90 days of salt spray test. According to Fig. 7(a), the area around the scratch and the entire surface area of the EP sample is completely covered by rust. The content of oxygen is as high as 34.8%, and the content of iron has decreased to 61.7%. The existence of a large number of rust products can be clearly observed in Fig. 7(a1). These results show that the EP coating has failed in corrosion protection. In contrast, although a large number of rust spots have appeared around the scratches of the EP/S-GQD (Fig. 7(b)) and EP/S-TNT (Fig. 7(c)) coatings after 90 days of salt spray test, less rust has appeared in other areas of the samples. Indeed, the corrosion is only serious around the scratches for the EP/S-GQD and EP/S-TNT coatings, and the coating surface has remained relatively intact. The amount of iron on the metallic substrate (i.e. under the peeled-off coating) of the EP/S-GQD and EP/S-TNT samples is 62.9% and 70.3%, while and the oxygen content is 21.7% and 23.3%, respectively, demonstrating that the solo-nanofillers have provided a degree of protective effect on the metallic substrates. The nanocomposite coatings containing nanohybrids have shown excellent anti-corrosion performance, and meanwhile, the nanocomposite coating loaded with S-H (1:1) has provided the highest corrosion resistance. As Fig. 7(d-f) represent, the coating protection efficiency in the area outside of the scratch is still very high. Fig. 7(d1-f1) and the related EDS diagrams clearly show less corrosion products; the main element is iron and the contents of other elements are very low, indicating that the coatings have not failed even after 90 days of salt spray test. Indeed, these nanocomposite coatings could effectively protect the metallic substrate from corrosion.

The results of the quantum chemical calculations using the DFT method are discussed to understand the mechanisms and the energetics of the interactions at the interfaces in the whole matrix. It should be noted that a strong interfacial junction between different components in the matrix is of great interest to achieve an efficient barrier against the corrosive agents.

The optimized geometries of the possible interfaces including (101) anatase TiO2-GQD, GQD-silane, and silane-epoxy resin are illustrated in Fig. 8 . Particularly, the dominant process of non-covalent bonding mediated by dispersion interactions was considered. As shown in Fig. 8(a), the GQD adsorbs in parallel with the surface of the TiO2, by a large adsorption energy of 690 kJ/mol. The minimum equilibrium distances between the GQD and the surface of the TNT is about 2.3 Å, which is in good agreement with the experimental results reported about the chemisorption of the GQD over the TiO2 [67]. Indeed, this chemisorption improves the dispersion of the nanoparticles during the synthesis process. More importantly, the barrier effect provided by this strong interface can be enhanced through blocking the possible diffusion paths for the corrosive agents to the metal surface. The distribution of the HOMO (the highest occupied molecular orbital) and LUMO (the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital) for the GQD (Fig. S8(a) in Supplementary Data) shows that these frontier orbitals are almost expanded over the entire of the GQD. As a result, the N–H functional group through the van der Waals forces can play a more crucial role in the adsorption over the surface of the TiO2. On the other hand, the COOH functional groups can interact electrostatically with the surface of the TiO2 through more electrostatic charges. As seen, the H and O atoms of the COOH functional groups interact with the O and Ti atoms from the TiO2, respectively. Thus, it is observed that both Van der Waals and electrostatics forces amplify the adhesion of the GQD over the surface of the TiO2.

Moving forwards, a strong interaction between GQD with the silane makes another interface in the matrix (Fig. 8(b)). This interface stabilizes by Ead of 279 kJ/mole, revealing the chemical adsorption of the silane over the GQD. Although this interface is looser than the previous case due to the presence of the metal surface, its amount of interaction energy is large enough to prevent the permeation of the corrosive agents to the metal surface [68]. The minimum equilibrium distances between the nearest H atom of the silane and the surface of the GQD is about 2.878 Å, from which the aromatic rings of the GQD and the H atoms from the silane interact via some C-H… π interactions. Note that, the CH…π interactions observed experimentally are shorter than 3.05 Å [69]. Moreover, the distribution of the HOMO and LUMO for this complex shows that the frontier orbitals are mainly expanded on the surface of the GQD; therefore, the attachment of the GQD to the surface of the TiO2 is more probable than that of the silane. This is in good agreement with our experimental results.

Respecting the previous sections, the silane as a functional group attached to the surface of the GQD can interact with the epoxy resin to increase the bonding strength between the reinforced nanofibers and the functional groups of the polymer. In order to better understand the issue, here, we have explored this interfacial junction. The most stable complex between the silane and the epoxy resin is illustrated in Fig. 8(c). The strong adsorption of the epoxy to the silane is observed by large adoption energy of 153 kJ/mole, indicating a chemical adsorption. According to our results, two main hydrogen bonds stabilize this complex; nitrogen atom from the silane and the hydrogen atom from the epoxy make a shorter distance of 1.890 Å as a hydrogen bond between the two monomers. In addition, oxygen atom from the epoxy resin interacts with hydrogen atom from the silane by an interaction distance of 2.504 Å. These interaction distances fall in the range of strong hydrogen bondings [70,71]. Overall, the energy values and the interaction distances obtained in this work show the chemisorption and, consequently, strong adhesion between the moieties. These strong adhesions are very important in polymer science and technology; indeed, the barrier effect against the corrosive agents can be enhanced, and thus, the metal surface is protected perfectly from the attack of these agents.

Fig. 9 shows the mechanisms proposed for the corrosion protection performance of the EP, EP/S-GQD, EP/S-TNT, and EP/S-H (1:1) coatings. The organic coatings can prevent the corrosive reagents (such as H2O, O2, and Cl-) from penetrating into the interface between the coating and the substrate, and reduce the damage of the metallic substrate [13,72]. The corrosive medium could pass through the coating to the substrate surface as the immersion time increases, and the anodic and cathodic electrochemical reactions mentioned in equations (2)–(7) could occur at the interface of the coating with the metallic substrate [73,74]:

For the EP coating, the protective performance of the EP coating is limited which is because of the defects and pores in its structure. As shown in Fig. 9(a), the corrosive medium could penetrate into the EP coating and reach to the surface of the carbon steel by prolonging the immersion time. Therefore, the electrochemical reactions will occur at the coating/metal interface, leading to the formation of the corrosion products and destroying the barrier effect of the EP coating. In the case of the steel substrate coated with the EP, the corrosion products on the surface of the carbon steel are mainly consist of FeOOH, FeCl2, or Fe2O3/Fe3O4, which is due to the presence of more corrosive media. When the steel substrate is coated with the EP/S-GQD coating (Fig. 9(b)) or EP/S-TNT coating (Fig. 9(c)), the addition of the APTES modified GQD or TNT can significantly improve the corrosion resistance of the epoxy matrix. The silane-modified nanomaterials can increase the length of the diffusion path for the corrosive agents and protect the carbon steel substrate. Moreover, the APTES modification could form a strong -O-Si covalent bonds between the nanomaterial surface and the coating. In addition, the amino groups of the APTES on the surface of the nanofillers can interact with the hydroxyl and epoxy groups of the epoxy coating, forming a strong polysiloxane network in the coating matrix, which increases the durability of the interface. Such a mechanism is also applicable to the nanocomposite coatings loaded with silane-modified nanohybrids: EP/S-H (1:0.5), EP/S-H (1:1), and EP/S-H (1:2) coatings. Fig. 9(d) schematically shows the corrosion protection mechanism of the EP/S-H (1:1) sample. The results have revealed that the bonding sites of the strong -O-Si bonds are increased because of the hybridization of nanomaterials. According to the corrosion studies, it has been shown that when the ratio of the GQD to the TNT is 1:1 in the nanohybrids, the anti-corrosion effect is the highest and the barrier degree of the coatings has increased notably. Therefore, the coatings loaded with the nanohybrid fillers could provide long-term resistance against the corrosion phenomenon.

Accordingly, to summarize the main effects of the nanomaterials used in these coating systems, the following points have been proved in this research:
•
Silane modification of the nanomaterials is an appropriate approach to improve the dispersion quality of the nanofillers in the coating matrix, to increase the chemical bonding between the nanofiller and the epoxy matrix, to decrease the defects and pores in the coating matrix, to enhance the adhesion strength of the coating to the metal surface, and to increase the corrosion resistance of the epoxy coating.
•
The nanohybrids prepared through hydrothermal reaction between the GQD and TNT could more significantly enhance the performance of the epoxy coatings. The ratio of the GQD to TNT in the synthesized nanohybrids has a key role in the features of the nanohybrids. The coatings loaded with the nanohybrids have high adhesion strength which is almost 2 times more than the coatings loaded with solo-nanofillers and almost 4 times more than the EP sample; indicating that the nanohybrids could provide more cohesive polymer matrix which has high adhesion strength to the metallic substrate.
•
Moreover, the nanocomposite coatings loaded with silane-modified nanohybrids have |Z|0.01Hz values in the range of 109 Ω·cm2 after 90 days of immersion in the 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, which is comparable with that of the EP (in the range of 104 Ω·cm2) and epoxy coatings loaded with S-GQD or S-TNT (in the range of 106 Ω·cm2). Indeed, the barrier effect of the polymer coating has improved through loading the nanohybrids in the coating matrix. It is attributed to the utilization of intrinsic properties of the nanomaterials in one system through synthesizing the nanohybrids.

In conclusion, this research reveals the importance of utilizing the advantages of the APTES modified GQD@TNT nanohybrids in the epoxy coatings to achieve long-term corrosion resistance. The results show that although S-GQD and S-TNT as solo-nanofillers could enhance the life-time of the epoxy coatings on the steel substrates, the silane- modified GQD@TNT nanohybrids could more significantly improve the barrier resistance and adhesion strength of the epoxy coatings. Meanwhile, the weight ratio of the GQD to the TNT in the synthesized nanohybrids has a key role in controlling the barrier effect of the nanocomposite coatings. Amongst the nanohybrids, the nanocomposite coatings loaded with the S-H (1:1) have the highest corrosion resistance during 90 days of corrosion studies. In addition, the quantum chemical results using the DFT method show that the strong adhesions at each interface highlight the positive effects of the epoxy coating because of twisting and increasing the diffusion path of the corrosive agents within the polymer matrix. Accordingly, when TNT is added to the GQD in the form of the nanohybrids, the barrier effect of the GQD has improved more significantly; while the adhesion strength of the nanocomposite coatings loaded with nanohybrids is almost 2 times more than the nanocomposites loaded with the solo-nanofillers. This research paves the way for producing nanocomposite coatings with long service life for industrial application.
